text: The surgeon prepares for a thymectomy by placing three incisions: a camera port in the fifth interspace, a right-handed port in the seventh interspace, and a left-handed port in the third interspace. Additionally, a subxiphoid incision is made to accommodate a twelve millimeter port for the robotic stapler, anticipating a potential wedge resection.

The surgeon places the camera in the chest, confirming an intrapleural position, and insufflates the chest. Under direct vision, additional ports are placed, including a subxiphoid port. The fascia is incised, and blunt dissection is used to extend the plane towards the ten or eleven o'clock position, with the camera guiding trocar placement.

The surgeon places a twelve millimeter trocar and then positions a trocar in the third interspace under thoracoscopic visualization, using a handheld robotic camera. Once the trocars are positioned, the robot is docked using the XI platform, with the arms aligned parallel to the trocars and the camera attached first.
The surgeon navigates the robotic arms between the third and fifth interspace ports, adjusting arm clearance buttons to manage the tight quarters within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper through the anterior twelve millimeter port to retract the mass, which is adherent to the right middle lobe. The no-touch technique is employed to handle the thymoma, ensuring minimal disturbance to the encapsulated tumor.

The surgeon uses a bipolar dissector through the seventh interspace port and a fenestrated bipolar through the third interspace port to perform a wedge resection of the adherent middle lobe of the lung. A 45mm Blue Load robotic stapler is employed to excise the lung tissue while keeping it attached to the tumor, minimizing contact with the mass to prevent pleural cavity seeding.
A dental roll with a radiopaque ribbon is used to gently retract the mass, minimizing the risk of fracturing the capsule during the thymectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses bipolar electrocautery to carefully dissect and separate the phrenic nerve from the surrounding tissue, sweeping it posteriorly away from the mass. The nerve appears uninvolved with the mass, as confirmed by preoperative imaging. The trainee cautiously applies energy while closing the jaws of the bipolar dissectors to ensure effective coagulation.
The surgeon begins to access the pericardium due to the large size of the mass, using robotic instruments to carefully dissect and remove a portion of the pericardium along with the thymic mass.

The surgeon identifies a small IIIa lymph node adjacent to the mass and prepares to remove it along with the specimen. A 30-degree scope is used to provide a versatile view, allowing observation of both the chest wall and areas beneath the tumor during dissection.

The surgeon visualizes the innominate vein and the right internal mammary vein while maneuvering a sponge. A thirty-degree scope is used to view the mediastinal fatty tissue, which is being dissected to ensure adequate margins around the thymoma. The pleura is separated from the chest wall, and the anterior mediastinal fat is divided below the tumor. The procedure remains anterior to the heart, minimizing the risk of phrenic nerve injury.

The surgeon carefully removes the dental roll, referred to as a 'peanut,' from the chest cavity to prevent accidental dropping. The contralateral lung becomes visible through the opposite pleura in the upper anterior mediastinum. The pleura is then opened, and the incision is extended from the upper mediastinum to the diaphragm, allowing visualization of the opposite phrenic nerve and facilitating specimen removal by placing the specimen bag into the opposite chest.
The surgeon searches for the right thymic horn, carefully cauterizing small arterial branches within the thymic tissue to prevent bleeding and retraction.
The surgeon uses the robotic fourth arm as a retractor to manage the bulkier thymic tumor, noting the absence of an assist port and bedside assistant during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the left thymic tissue and proceeds to divide small tributaries off the innominate vein. Smaller tributaries are cauterized, while larger ones are tied, with options to use hemlock clips or five millimeter clips.

The surgeon removes the tip-up grasper through the twelve millimeter port, and the scrub tech passes a two-oh silk ligature into the surgical field. The tip-up grasper is reinserted to provide cephalad retraction on the thymic tissue, enhancing visualization of the thymic vein. The surgeon checks the course of the phrenic nerve using a thirty degree downward view, confirming that the dissection is well away from the nerve.

The surgeon carefully holds the thymic mass with graspers to prevent it from slipping into the deeper chest cavity. The retraction instrument is removed, and the subxiphoid incision is enlarged to extract the mass through the subxiphoid port. A bag is placed into the left chest to facilitate pushing the mass into it without directly pressing on the tumor, avoiding capsular tear and potential seeding of the pleural space.

The surgeon extracts the specimen by slightly enlarging the subxiphoid incision, revealing the mass with two thymic horns and the pericardium. A 24 French chest tube is inserted before closing the fascia.
